segments.title: How Hawker rebuilt Sopwith's aviation capability
segments.summary: The postwar collapse of Sopwith leads into Hawker's formation and the survival of an engineering team that remained important to British naval aviation.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke traces how excess-profits taxation, falling demand, and surplus wartime aircraft pushed Sopwith into receivership despite its First World War importance. Hawker then emerged from the same premises, people, and engineering inheritance, eventually growing into a major interwar group. The continuity helps explain why Hawker and Supermarine mattered to Fleet Air Arm development as well as to the RAF.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Sopwith's receivership, Hawker's formation, later consolidation, and the firms' naval-aviation importance.

segments.title: The Hedgehog as Hawker's first naval-aircraft attempt
segments.summary: Hawker's three-seat Hedgehog met a fleet reconnaissance requirement but offered too little improvement over aircraft already entering service.
segments.body: The Hedgehog was a large, folding-wing fleet reconnaissance biplane designed against an Admiralty requirement issued through the Air Ministry. Although the prototype was purchased for further tests, the requirement was cancelled because its performance did not sufficiently surpass the Bison, Blackburn, and Panther. The episode shows an early Hawker naval design teaching useful lessons without securing production.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the Hedgehog's naval role, folded wings, performance, specification, and cancelled requirement.

segments.title: The Horsley and the land-based torpedo-bomber idea
segments.summary: The large Hawker Horsley evolved through changing bomber specifications into an RAF answer for attacking battleships with land-based torpedo aircraft.
segments.body: Originally conceived as a medium bomber, the Horsley became a land-based torpedo aircraft through several rounds of specification changes between 1923 and 1927. Its size, endurance, torpedo load, and repeated engine installations illustrate how interwar aircraft often had a much longer and more iterative gestation than a single specification date suggests. The airframe later served as a test bed for early Rolls-Royce Merlin development.

evidence.note: The transcript covers the Horsley's bomber origins, torpedo role, specification changes, load, endurance, and engine testing.

segments.title: The Hoopoe exposes naval-fighter procurement constraints
segments.summary: The experimental Hoopoe frames why the Admiralty struggled to specify high-performance naval fighters without its own aviation engineering staff.
segments.body: Hawker repeatedly changed the Hoopoe's engines, wings, cooling, and undercarriage while testing whether a heavily equipped radial-engined naval fighter could achieve competitive performance. Dr. Clarke uses the aircraft to explain an institutional problem: the Admiralty depended on the Air Ministry to define what aviation technology was feasible. That arrangement made the Navy an imperfectly informed customer and encouraged conservative requirements shaped by another service's technical assumptions.

evidence.note: The transcript follows the Hoopoe's development and connects it to Admiralty dependence on Air Ministry expertise.

segments.title: From Hawker Hart to Osprey fleet air defence
segments.summary: A Hart-derived seaplane becomes the Osprey, helping the Royal Navy develop fighter-control tactics and expose the limits of visual warning before radar.
segments.body: The Hart was adapted with folding wings, floats, and corrosion resistance for naval trials, eventually producing the Osprey after a procurement process split between two ministries. In service from carriers and cruisers, Ospreys helped the Fleet Air Arm experiment with interception using visual spotting, radio telegraphy, aircraft reconnaissance, and destroyer pickets. The mixed results demonstrated both the value and unreliability of fighter interception, contributing to the thinking behind protected carriers such as the Illustrious class.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the Hart conversion, Osprey specification, trials, and two-ministry procurement delay.
The transcript covers Osprey service, corrosion-resistant construction, fighter-control experiments, and carrier-design influence.

segments.title: The Nimrod as the Osprey's carrier-fighter partner
segments.summary: The Hawker Nimrod entered carrier service alongside the Osprey and equipped several Royal Navy fighter squadrons during the interwar years.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke places the Nimrod beside the Osprey as the Fleet Air Arm's complementary Hawker fighter types of the early 1930s. Nimrods served aboard carriers including HMS Glorious and were also exported to Denmark, Japan, and Portugal. The aircraft marks a more purposeful carrier-fighter capability than Hawker's earlier experimental naval designs.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Nimrod carrier service, Royal Navy squadrons, foreign operators, and its relationship to the Osprey.

segments.title: Sea Hurricanes bring fighter defence to convoys
segments.summary: Sea Hurricane conversions move from one-way CAM-ship launches to MAC ships and fleet carriers, giving threatened shipping a fighter-based air-defence option.
segments.body: The Sea Hurricane answered the Battle of the Atlantic problem by adapting RAF fighters for shipboard use, first on catapult aircraft merchantmen whose pilots could not return to the launching ship. MAC ships added small flight decks but no hangars, making aircraft maintenance especially difficult on Arctic convoys. Carrier-based Sea Hurricanes also fought during the August 1942 Malta convoys, where naval fighters disrupted sustained German and Italian air attacks.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Sea Hurricane conversion and the CAM-ship fighter-defence concept.
The transcript covers MAC ships, Arctic maintenance, Sea Hurricane variants, and Malta convoy combat.

segments.title: Sea Fury orders sustain Hawker after the war
segments.summary: The Fury's evolution from the Tempest produced a major Fleet Air Arm order and a piston fighter that remained effective in Korea against ground targets and early jets.
segments.body: A lighter Tempest concept evolved into the Sea Fury through intensive naval trials, entering Royal Navy service as the FB.11 after the war. The Fleet Air Arm's large order helped sustain Hawker while the aircraft served from British and Australian carriers in Korea, attacking ground targets and engaging MiG-15s. Its career illustrates why mature piston technology remained valuable while early jet aircraft and their operating practices were still developing.

evidence.note: The transcript traces the Typhoon-Tempest-Fury lineage, naval trials, Fleet Air Arm order, and Hawker's postwar continuity.
The transcript describes Sea Fury carrier service and combat in Korea, including engagements with MiG-15s.
